1: vayeviʾeni ʾel-heikhal — the guide brings Ezekiel into the heikhal (nave/main hall of the temple), the outer of the two main rooms. Heikhal is borrowed from the Sumerian egal (great house) via Akkadian — it denotes both the palace of a king and the main room of a temple. The doorpost-pillars (ʾeil) measured at six cubits: ʾeil (doorpost/jamb) is literally a ram or pillar — the same word used for the temple entrance in 1 Kings 6:31. The measurement formula (vayyamed ʾet-ʾeil hapetha sita ʾammot) begins the systematic numerical description of the sanctuary zones.
2: The entrance (petaḥ) ten cubits wide and the sides of the entrance five cubits each — the petaḥ (opening/doorway) of the nave. The word katef (shoulder/side) used for the flanking walls establishes the architectural spatial language: the entrance has a width (roḥav) and two shoulders (katfot). The description moves inward: doorposts → entrance → nave walls.
3: veheikhal penim — the inner room, the Most Holy Place (devir by implication though not named here). The guide measures the jambs at two cubits, the doorway at six cubits, and the walls seven cubits on each side. The narrowing of the entrance (petaḥ shisha ʾammot vs. the nave entrance of ten cubits) mirrors the increasing sanctity as one moves inward — architecturally encoding the graduated holiness of the temple zones.
4: vayyamed ʾet-orko ʿesrim ʾammah veʾet-roḥbo ʿesrim ʾammah — the innermost room is precisely 20 × 20 cubits, a perfect square. The cube/square of the Most Holy Place is its defining architectural feature (also 1 Kings 6:20, where it is 20 × 20 × 20 cubits — a perfect cube). The geometric perfection encodes the absolute holiness of the space. The declaration zeh qodesh haqqodashim (this is the Most Holy Place) at the end of v4 is the interpretive climax of the architectural description of the inmost sanctuary.
5: The outer wall of the temple measured at six cubits, and the side chambers (tsela) four cubits wide: tsela literally means "rib/side" (as in Eve from Adam's rib, Genesis 2:21-22). The side chambers running along the temple walls were storage rooms and priestly spaces. The three-story arrangement of the side chambers (v6) is the same structural element described in 1 Kings 6:5-6.
6: The thirty-three side chambers in three stories — tslaʿot tsela ʾel-tsela shalosh ushloshim peʿamim — a total of 33 per side (the Hebrew is debated; LXX has a different reading). The widening of the upper stories (hammisgeret) so they do not penetrate the temple wall is the structural innovation that allows multi-story side chambers without compromising the temple's main wall integrity.
7: The widening by stages as one goes higher — veroḥav yereveh umussav saviv saviv — the winding passage (musav) that circled the temple on each level. The architectural term musav (winding/surrounding passage) appears only here and is related to savav (to go around). The structural widening by story reflects the load-bearing engineering of ancient multi-story stone construction.
8: The raised platform (gabhut lahem ḥatser) — the elevated terrace surrounding the temple. The measurement formula specifies the height of the terrace: shesh ʾammot ʾatsum (six cubits by full measure). ʾatsum (full/complete) may modify the cubit as a standard unit, distinguishing it from the short cubit (seven handbreadths vs. six) noted elsewhere in Ezekiel (Ezekiel 40:5).
9: The wall of the side chambers — five cubits — and the free space (manuaḥ) left open between the side chambers and the priests' chambers (lishkot siv-tsela habbayit). Manuaḥ literally means "resting place/open space" (from nuaḥ, to rest). The architectural principle of leaving open space between structures is both practical and symbolic in the sanctuary context.
10: The space between the chambers — twenty cubits around the temple. This free space (the gizrah of v12) created a buffer between the sanctuary and the outer courts. The structural principle of graduated separation between zones of holiness is built into the architecture of the vision.
11: The doorways of the side chambers facing the free space — petaḥ hatsela ʾel-hammanuaḥ — one door to the north and one to the south. The orientation of the entrances toward the manuaḥ (free space) rather than toward the outer court is a deliberate boundary-maintaining feature: access to the side chambers is from the sanctuary-side, not from the public court-side.
12: habbinyan ʾasher lifnei haggizrah — the building (binyan) before the separated yard (gizrah) on the west side: seventy cubits wide, ninety cubits long, with five-cubit walls. Gizrah (separated area/restricted zone) is a technical architectural term derived from gazar (to cut off/separate). The western building with its separated yard completes the overall plan of the sanctuary compound.
13: The overall temple measurements: one hundred cubits long and one hundred cubits wide — the perfect square of the outer boundary. The overall compound is thus a perfect square, with the inner most room (20 × 20) as its core. The numerical symbolism of 100 × 100 (= 10² × 10²) encodes divine perfection at the macroscopic level of the entire complex.
14: The width of the east face of the temple — one hundred cubits — and the separated area: the breadth of the face toward the east is established as the final measurement completing the outer boundary. The repeated use of the word phanim (face/front) for the east side marks the temple's orientation: the east is the phanim (front/face) because YHWH's glory entered from the east (Ezekiel 43:1-4).
15: The building behind the temple including its galleries: one hundred cubits. The term ʾattiq (gallery/colonnade) is a technical architectural term of uncertain etymology; it appears to refer to the covered colonnades running along the sides of the inner court. The measurement confirms the overall square footprint of the complex.
16: The thresholds (sippim), latticed windows (ḥallonot ʾatumat), and galleries (ʾatiqot) round about on three sides — paneled with wood from the floor to the windows. Ḥallonot ʾatumat (narrow/closed windows) is the same term used for Solomon's temple windows (1 Kings 6:4). The covering (paneling) with wood from floor to ceiling is the sanctuary's interior finish. Saf (threshold/floor-level) establishes the starting point for the wood-paneling measurement.
17: The space above the door and to the inner chamber and to the outside — measurements given. The spatial description moves carefully from the measured threshold up to the carved surfaces. The wood-paneling program is the context for the carvings described in vv18-20.
18: ʿasuyi keruvim vetimmorot — made with cherubim (keruvim) and palm trees (timmorot). The alternating cherub-palm decorative motif is the same as Solomon's temple interior (1 Kings 6:29, 32, 35). Each cherub has two faces: penei ʾadam ʾel-hattimorat mizzeh upenei kefir ʾel-hattimorat mizzeh — the face of a man and the face of a young lion alternating on each side of the palm. This is a simplified version of the four-faced cherubim of Ezekiel 1 and 10.
19: The two faces of the wall carvings — human face to the palm on one side, lion face to the palm on the other. The reduction from four faces (Ezekiel 1) to two faces on the wall carvings represents the adaptation of the vision-cherubim for architectural use. The facing-pairs (human/palm, lion/palm) suggest bilateral symmetry and the integration of creation-order (human authority and leonine power) with the garden-axis of the sanctuary (the palm as stylized tree-of-life).
20: The cherubim and palm trees carved from the floor to above the doorway — mehaʾaretz ʿad-meʿal hapetaḥ — on the entire wall of the nave. The continuous carved program from floor to lintel transforms the nave interior into a visual garden-sanctuary, continuous with the garden-imagery of Eden. The same motif decorates both the nave (heikhal) and the Most Holy Place entrance, linking the two zones visually.
21: hamezonah mezuzot hamishkan — the doorposts of the nave are square: mezuzat (doorpost, the structural element that receives the mezuzah-scroll in Deuteronomy 6:9) combined with mishkan (tabernacle/dwelling-place). The specific term for the nave's doorposts is architecturally precise; the square doorpost form distinguishes the nave from the curved/rounded entryways of ordinary buildings.
22: hamizbeaḥ ʿets shalosh ʾammot ghovah... veqirot mimmenu ʿatsayim — the wooden altar: three cubits high, two cubits long — veqirot mimmenu ʿatsayim (its walls of wood). The angel identifies it: zeh hashulḥan ʾasher lifnei YHWH — "this is the table that is before YHWH." The wooden altar is explicitly identified as a shulḥan (table) rather than an altar for burnt offerings. This is the incense altar/table of presence, not a sacrificial altar. The three terms — altar dimensions, wooden construction, identification as table before YHWH — distinguish it clearly from the great bronze altar of v13.
23: The two doors of the nave and of the Most Holy Place: ushetayim daletot lahekhal veladevir. The term devir (inner oracle/Most Holy Place) appears here — it is the term used in 1 Kings 6:5, 16, 19-23 for the innermost chamber. The use of devir here (not just "inner room") anchors the visionary temple to the Solomonic temple vocabulary.
24: The double doors (delatayim — a dual of delet, door) with two swinging/folding leaves (tsalmot) on each door: two leaves on one door and two leaves on the other. Tsalmot (folding leaves, from tselem or possibly related to tsalal, to fold/collapse) allows the doors to swing open to either side. The precision of the architectural vocabulary shows the visionary guidance takes the temple engineering seriously.
25: Cherubim and palm trees carved on the nave doors as on the walls — the decorative program is continuous between walls and doors. The wooden canopy (ʿets ʿav) before the entrance of the nave: ʿab (thick/dense) applied to wood suggests a dense carved wooden screen or a carved lintel. The threshold (saf) at the entrance completes the architectural frame.
26: The narrow windows and palm trees on either side of the porch, including the side chambers — the carved program extends from the inner spaces outward to the porch. The consistency of the palm-tree motif throughout the temple complex (nave walls, doors, portico, side chambers) creates a unified visual theology: the sanctuary is the garden, and the garden is the place of divine presence.
